Quick reference for fitness and nutrition projects
explanation:
**Nutrition and fitness** are based on physical principles (energy balance) and biological principles (macronutrients, micronutrients). This calculator uses formulas validated by the scientific community (ACSM, ISSN, NIH).

## How It Calculates

Plant-based protein and vegan diet macros.

This calculator applies the formula with the values you enter, checking typical ranges and alerting you to values outside reasonable parameters.

## Standard Caloric Distribution

| Macronutrient | % Calories | Grams/kcal |
|---|---|---|
| Protein | 15-35% | 4 kcal/g |
| Carbohydrates | 45-65% | 4 kcal/g |
| Fats | 20-35% | 9 kcal/g |
| Alcohol | — | 7 kcal/g |
| Fiber | Minimum 25-38g/day | — |

## When to Consult a Nutritionist

- If you have medical conditions (diabetes, hypertension, kidney disease) requiring personalized nutrition.
- If you want significant muscle gain or athletic competition.
- If you struggle with disordered eating (binge eating, extreme restriction).
- If you've been working toward your goal for more than 3 months without progress.
- For comprehensive plant-based or vegetarian meal planning.

## Final Notes

This calculation is an informational reference. For critical decisions (financial, medical, legal), consult a qualified professional. Values are current to 2026 and reviewed regularly.

faq.q: How many grams of protein do vegans need daily?
faq.a: Sedentary: 0.8g/kg body weight. Active: 1.2-1.5g/kg. Muscle building: 1.6-2.2g/kg lean mass. Effective ceiling ~2.2g/kg.

faq.q: What's the best macro ratio for a vegan diet?
faq.a: Standard: Protein 15-35%, Carbs 45-65%, Fats 20-35% of calories. Adjust based on your goals: more protein for muscle gain, more carbs for endurance.

faq.q: How do I get enough protein on a plant-based diet?
faq.a: Combine legumes (beans, lentils, peas), tofu, tempeh, nuts, seeds, whole grains, and plant-based protein powder. Variety ensures complete amino acid profiles.

faq.q: Can you build muscle on a vegan diet?
faq.a: Yes. Plant-based protein, adequate calories, strength training, and proper recovery work. Many athletes thrive on vegan nutrition.
Vitamin B12 (critical), Vitamin D, Omega-3 (algae), and iron if at risk. Creatine can help athletic performance. Whey-free protein powders are available.

faq.q: How many calories do I need daily on a vegan diet?
faq.a: Depends on age, sex, weight, and activity level. Average adult female: 1800-2200 kcal; adult male: 2200-3000 kcal. Use TDEE calculator for precision.

faq.q: Is a vegan diet high in carbs?
faq.a: It can be if you rely on refined grains and processed foods. Focus on whole grains, legumes, vegetables, and healthy fats for balanced macros.

faq.q: What are complete protein sources for vegans?
faq.a: Quinoa, buckwheat, hemp seeds, chia seeds, soy products (tofu, tempeh, edamame), nutritional yeast, and legume-grain combinations (rice + beans).

sources.name: NIST — National Institute of Standards and Technology

dataUpdate.notes: Formula is fixed and does not require external data updates
